result:
I want to talk about how absurdly, beautifully weird it is to be a conscious thing hurtling through space on a wet rock.

Right now, as I type this, there are billions of humans walking around with squidgy 1.4 kg brains that somehow generate color, music, grief, jokes, and the sudden 3 a.m. craving for instant ramen. We put on pants (or don’t), argue about pineapple on pizza, send probes to other planets, and cry at sunsets like the universe personally wrote us a love letter in orange and violet.

We are the only species we know of that can look at a mountain and feel small, then immediately feel enormous because we’re the ones capable of feeling small in the first place. That recursive loop of awareness is completely insane. The universe spent 13.8 billion years doing its fiery, gravitational, nuclear thing and somehow arrived at creatures who write songs about how sad they are that flowers die.

I love the ridiculous specificity of it all. Someone, somewhere, is stress-baking sourdough at 2 a.m. while crying to Mitski. Another person is inventing a new knot because they sail tall ships for fun. A third is live-streaming their pet snail’s slow journey across a LEGO brick. None of this was on the agenda when the first cyanobacteria figured out photosynthesis. We are the universe’s chaotic, self-aware Rube Goldberg machine.

And the best part? None of us really know what we’re doing. We’re all just guessing, making it up as we go, trying to be kind (or at least not too much of an asshole) before the lights go out. Every friendship, every stupid inside joke, every time you stayed up all night talking about nothing important with someone you love… that’s the point. That’s the glitch in the code that makes the whole simulation worth running.

The older I get, the more I think the meaning of life might just be: *pay attention*. Notice how good cold water tastes when you’re really thirsty. Notice how someone’s laugh changes when they’re trying not to cry. Notice that you’re a temporary pattern of atoms that learned how to miss people before they’re gone.

Anyway.

Right now the sun is coming through my window at a perfect angle and there’s a house finch screaming its tiny red heart out in the tree outside. I don’t know what any of us are supposed to do with this wild, brief, heartbreaking gift of consciousness.

But I’m glad we get to be ridiculous about it together.

What’s something small and stupid and wonderful that made you happy today?
